Recent uncertainties affecting the market include the Nigerian authorities' revocation in July 2023 of Orano's permit to exploit the Imouraren deposit, followed in June 2024 by the revocation of GaviEx Uranium (Canada-based) for the Madaouela project, creating significant uncertainty regarding future production centers.

Leaving aside these unforeseen events, many producers have expanded production in recent years. At the same time, governments are developing new frameworks in countries that previously had no uranium production. It should be noted that opening or expanding a uranium mine is not a simple undertaking and requires a lot of time, with many variables that can slow down the launch of these projects.

All investor presentations by uranium-related companies state that there is a gap between production and demand, which will lead to an increase in the price of uranium. However, as indicated by the Nuclear Energy Agency (NEA), this gap between production and demand has existed since 1990 and has been consistently filled by secondary supplies, primarily consisting of reprocessed fuel, stockpiles, and disarmament materials from nuclear weapons programs. The NEA report highlights that although this gap has begun to close in recent years, projections indicate that it could widen again, given the expected increase in demand in the coming years. Although the presence of inventories, including military stockpiles, will continue to represent an element of uncertainty for future investments, reactor requirements will increasingly need to be met by primary production.

In its latest report (Uranium 2024: Resources, Production, and Demand), the NEA identifies two possible scenarios:

  • Low-production scenario: If all existing and committed mines operate at or near their stated production capacity, low demand is expected to be met until 2031. However, for high demand, a production shortfall is anticipated to emerge around 2027.
  • High-production scenario: If planned and prospective production capabilities are included, high demand could be met by primary production until 2037. In this scenario, low demand would be satisfied until 2042.

Uranium Demand

As of January 1, 2023, there were 438 commercial nuclear reactors in operation worldwide and 58 under construction. A significant increase in the number of nuclear reactors is expected in the coming decades, which are essential for producing a stable, low-cost energy source and reducing emissions. In 2022, the European Commission included nuclear energy in its EU taxonomy, classifying it as an environmentally sustainable investment that will help decarbonize the European Union's economy. The countries investing most heavily in nuclear power are India and China. Alongside these developments are the US, which remains the largest user of reactors; France, which is heavily dependent on nuclear power and has launched strategies to maintain and renew its existing fleet; and Japan, which is gradually bringing back into operation the reactors shut down after Fukushima.

In the short-term, the quantities of uranium required for reactor operation can be predicted with a high degree of certainty. Generally, higher uranium prices have encouraged utilities to reduce uranium requirements by specifying lower tails assays at enrichment facilities, to the extent possible. In recognition of these market trends, uranium requirements for the operational lifetime of projected new reactors have been reduced from 175 tU/GWe/yr to 160 tU/GWe/yr (from a 0.30% to a 0.25% tails assay). When determining the quantities of uranium that will be needed, it should also be taken into account that first load fuel requirements are around 60% higher than reloads for plants in operation. In addition, the excellent performance of currently operating reactors has led to a trend toward extending their useful life. The tables below show the NEA's estimates for installed capacity and the quantities of uranium needed to meet it until 2050. In 2050, installed capacity is expected to be 2.3 times that of 2021, and the uranium needed is expected to be about 2.4 times that of 2022.

One factor that introduces uncertainty into these forecasts is undoubtedly possible technological developments, such as the future adoption of fourth-generation reactors, which promise to be able to use waste from old power plants as fuel. However, the reactors currently under construction use “traditional” technologies that have been in use for decades and are known to be reliable.

Conclusion

Yellow Cake is an excellent way to invest in uranium in a “pure” way, without exposure to the operational risks typical of mining companies. In light of the outlook for uranium supply and demand in the coming years, it is reasonable to assume a structural increase in the price of this metal, positively influencing the value of Yellow Cake's reserves and its NAV. However, given the absence of cash flows, it is extremely difficult to value the company, and in my opinion, this does not make it a good option for a classic long-term investment.

Another risk to consider is that it is unclear what will happen after the contract with Kazatomprom expires at the end of 2027. Yellow Cake may find it difficult to negotiate a new supply agreement, meaning that the company's uranium reserves could remain stable. Clearly, this does not reduce the value of the company, but the mechanism adopted so far of issuing shares when their price is above NAV in order to buy more uranium could be difficult to continue.

In conclusion, considering current reserves, the GBP/USD exchange rate, the price of uranium, and Yellow Cake's share price at the time of writing, the share price is trading at a discount of approximately 9% to NAV.

data.title: PDI: I Remain Bullish About This Income Fund
data.description:
Since I started covering the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (NYSE:PDI), there has been a bear case discussing the liquidity of PDI’s distribution and that they wouldn’t be able to maintain it throughout an inflationary environment. PDI has ridden out the storm, is up 2.04% over the past year, and has paid a monthly distribution of $0.23 since September 2015. When I look for income-producing assets I am on the hunt for quality income that comes with a strong long-term investment case. PIMCO is a global leader in active fixed income, and it is much easier to gain exposure to these markets through PDI and let their management team do the work. I believe that shares of PDI will continue to generate large amounts of recurring income and as the macroeconomic environment shifts PDI’s underlying assets should experience significant appreciation as bond prices should increase as yields fall. Another positive is that since PDI is a leveraged fund it should benefit from improved borrowing costs as the Fed lowers rates. Many investors have been waiting a long time for Fed Chair Powell to pivot and in the latest speech from Jackson Hole I believe we received the signal that the Fed will shift on monetary policy in September. As the risk-free rate of return starts to decline again allocating capital toward risk-free assets such as money markets and CDs will look less appealing and PDI’s distribution yield of 13.61% will look very enticing. I am long PDI, and reinvesting the monthly income has allowed me to organically build out a stronger income stream each month.

Following up on my previous article about PDI

Since my previous article about PDI (can be read here) shares have appreciated by 5.3% while the S&P 500 has increased by 11.31%. When the monthly distributions are accounted for PDI’s total return over this period has been 9.01%. I had discussed why PDI’s 14% yield was still attractive despite its share price declines. I felt that PDI’s focus on agency mortgages and dynamic asset allocation provided a level of resilience and that its actively managed fixed-income portfolio is well-positioned for a rebound. PDI continued to deliver large amounts of income on a monthly basis, and I am more bullish today than I previously was. We are one step closer to rate cuts now that Fed Chair Powell delivered his address from Jackson Hole and I am following up with a new article to discuss why I feel PDI is going to rally into the end of the year and continue pumping out large amounts of income.

Risks to investing in PDI

While I am bullish on PDI, there are several risk factors that investors should consider. PDI is a leveraged CEF, which makes it sensitive to interest rates, credit events, and macroeconomic shifts. If, for some reason, something occurs in the data to make the Fed change their stance on monetary policy, it could be very negative for PDI as it could lead to declines in bond prices and the underlying NAV. PDI employs a leverage ratio that exceeds 30% and in a higher-rate environment the chances of its underlying holdings experiencing higher rates of defaults or credit downgrades increase significantly. If companies in their portfolio experience hardships, then PDI’s distribution could face coverage shortfalls as some of its assets could turn illiquid and disrupt the strong distribution yield. Investors should do their own due diligence as it’s difficult to research PDI’s underlying assets and a lot of faith is being placed on the management team. Investors should also be aware that PDI focuses on fixed income, and its share price is unlikely to replicate the returns of an S&P 500 index fund.

Fed Chair Powell just signaled that rate cuts are coming in my opinion and that is bullish for PDI

In Fed Chair Powell's speech from 8/22, he addressed the economic situation and the near-term outlook for monetary policy. Fed Chair Powell stated that “with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance.” Throughout the address he discussed the current economic situation and the near-term outlook for monetary policy, but all roads led back to the idea that adjusting their policy stance may be necessary. This was enough for CME Group to update the chances of a rate cut at the September meeting to 86.2% while getting 2 rate cuts by the end of the year increased to 48.3% and getting 3 rate cuts increased to 37.3%. Looking out to the end of 2026, the most likely scenario is that rates would fall 225 bps from where they are today which is super bullish for PDI. This would create a night and day difference in the macroeconomic environment which would have drastic implications for fixed income.

If we do get a prolonged Fed cutting cycle, I think that we’re going to see PDI’s share price get back to the mid-20s. PDI predominantly invests in global fixed income securities which include high-yield bonds, mortgage-backed securities (MBS), emerging market debt, and other credit instruments. When interest rates decline the prices of existing fixed income securities typically increase because their higher coupon payments become more attractive relative to newly issued bonds with lower yields. PDI’s portfolio includes a mix of investment-grade and below investment-grade bonds that would benefit from the inverse relationship between interest rates and bond prices. An ongoing cutting cycle like the one CME Group is projecting would likely result in a sustained bond rally that would increase PDI’s NAV over time. PDI’s portfolio also has interest rate-sensitive assets such as MBS and corporate bonds, and a lower rate environment would reduce the discount rates used to value future cash flow.

PDI has a large allocation toward bonds which are debt securities that pay a fixed interest rate over their term. The yield on a bond is the return an investor will earn through its payments, which is calculated as the annual coupon payment divided by the bond's current market price. When interest rates fall the yield on newly issued bonds decreases because they are priced to reflect the lower rate environment. PDI’s portfolio of existing bonds will continue paying their higher coupon rates which makes them more attractive to investors compared to new bonds with lower yields. The inverse relationship will cause older bonds that traded at a discount to increase in value as investors buy them until their effective yield correlates to newly issued bonds. I believe that this will take PDI’s share price into the mid $20 range over the next 6-12 months. When I look at PDI’s share performance, there is a direct correlation to a higher rate environment and PDI’s share price falling. I am expecting a breakout to occur as rates fall under 4% to the upside.

PDI’s distribution yield is very lucrative and much more effective than traditional income funds

I believe that PIMCO has built a better income machine through PDI than many investment vehicles on the market. PDI went IPO for $25 in 2012, and since then it has produced $41.62 of distribution income which is 166.47% of its share price. Not only have investors received their entire investment back, but they have also generated an additional $16.62 per share in distributions while the shares are still producing $2.65 of annualized income. This is where my income-focused investing style comes into play because I am not concerned about a -22.08% drop overtime in the share price considering that the investment has already been paid for from the income for long-term investors. It’s hard to get upset about a fluctuating share price that is spending a few years under its IPO price when the main investment objective is to generate recurring distribution income. PDI has never cut the distribution and provided investors with several raises over the years. I still think this is a great opportunity to lock in a 13.61% distribution yield, as I believe PDI’s share price will increase as the Fed lowers rates.

There is less reason from an income perspective to invest in an ETF such as the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYM) that is yielding 2.54% with an average 5-year dividend growth rate of 3.59%. If I were to put $100,000 into VYM, I could purchase 719.22 shares. If I held it for 6 years, I would generate $2,538.84 of dividend income in year 1 and the dividend would grow to $4.21, which is an increase of 19.29% ($0.68). The annual dividend would increase to $3,023.69, and over the 6-year period of the $100,000 investment there would be $16,645.10 of dividend income generated. PDI, on the other hand, would generate $13,603.70 of distribution income off a $100,000 investment in the first year and if the distribution doesn’t increase over the 6-year period PDI would generate $81,622.18 in distributions. I think there is less reason to purchase lower-yielding equity-based ETFs because from an income perspective, the dividend growth from VYM doesn’t correlate to enough income generated to get excited. I think that as the Fed lowers rates, we’re going to see a lot of capital move into funds like PDI because of their income-generating potential and I am adding to my position before the share price gets back into the $20s.

Conclusion

Fed Chair Powell has spoken, and he clearly indicated that adjusting their policy stance is on the table due to the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks. This is very bullish for PDI because most of its underlying assets will greatly benefit causing their NAV to increase and pushing PDI’s share price higher. I think that shares of PDI have the potential to get back into the mid $20s as the Fed takes its rate slower while maintaining their current distribution rate. From an income perspective, there is less reason to look at low single-digit yielding ETFs for income compared to a fund like PDI that is comprised of global fixed income assets with double-digit yields. I think that PDI can generate large amounts of recurring income and large monthly distributable income for its shareholders, and I am very bullish heading into the remainder of 2025.
